summary: This episode traces the evolution of NIH's Normal Control Volunteer Program from the 1950s through the 1970s, examining how recruitment shifted from religious conscientious objectors to college students and eventually to transient, paid participants and incarcerated individuals. As the program expanded to find "more normal" subjects, motivations shifted from altruism to financial incentive, safeguards diminished, and the program came to resemble a gig economy. The episode highlights the tragic death of nursing student Bernadette Gilchrist in 1980 during a sleep study, which exemplified the vulnerabilities of the new pay-per-study model and lack of institutional oversight.
science_topics: Normal human physiology research and control group methodology
Drug testing on healthy human subjects
Flu vaccine efficacy and vaccine contamination incidents
Eating disorder and cardiac physiology (in context of Bernadette Gilchrist case)
claims_to_note: NIH researchers used LSD in psychological studies on healthy volunteers in the 1950s-1960s without clear justification or apparent scientific rigor
NIH conducted intentional infection experiments on federal prisoners between 1960-1964, including experiments with a flu vaccine contaminated with a virus suspected of causing brain cancer
A flu vaccine culture used in prisoner experiments was contaminated with a virus thought to cause brain cancer, and NIH researchers then studied the prisoners after administration

Bernadette Gilchrist, a 23-year-old nursing student with documented history of eating disorder and prior myocardial infarction related to that disorder, was not identified as high-risk during sleep study enrollment despite having prior NIH medical records
Bernadette Gilchrist's death in the sleep lab went unnoticed until after the experiment concluded, suggesting inadequate monitoring protocols
The program recruited approximately 1,000 incarcerated men from at least 12 different federal sites between 1960-1964 for virus experiments
policy_relevance: NIH recruitment and contracting practices for human subjects research evolved from formal institutional partnerships (peace churches) to informal pay-per-study arrangements

Antioch College successfully negotiated with NIH to allow student volunteers to attend political protests, while NIH ultimately terminated the contract when the institution would not comply with restrictions on student freedom of speech
Federal funding cuts in the 1970s to U.S. and UK research budgets impacted NIH contracting models and shifted from salaried positions to hourly/gig-economy arrangements for research subjects

NIH began formal arrangements to use federal prisoners as research subjects, fundamentally changing the ethics of recruitment from voluntary civilians to incarcerated populations without full legal rights

The death of Bernadette Gilchrist and conditions in the sleep lab are implied to have prompted future policy changes to human subject protections, with additional details promised in the final episode
Medical record-keeping failures at NIH regarding Bernadette Gilchrist's prior cardiac history and eating disorder
NIH's use of incarcerated subjects had documented global impact on international human subject protection standards
key_quotes: The only people who wept from being there in the 1950s were the normals who were there because they were having a religious experience... The only people who actually didn't remember doing it... were like 'oh yeah, I guess that was something that I did.' It was just, you know, like a lark or like the umpteenth college job that they had to have. There was this remarkable difference in the meaning that people attributed between the 1950s and the 1970s.
The normals were just practice material for the researchers. And so one of the huge values of the normals for NIH is they just got populations for these trainees to just practice on.
I think many relationships are transactional... But in practice, I realized that there really needed to be more protection so that the motivations of each member were supported safely.

publications.title: Scientific and Medical Aspects of Human Reproductive Cloning
publications.description: Human reproductive cloning is an assisted reproductive technology that would be carried out with the goal of creating a newborn genetically identical to another human being. It is currently the subject of much debate around the world, involving a variety of ethical, religious, societal, scientific, and medical issues. Scientific and Medical Aspects of Human Reproductive Cloning considers the scientific and medical sides of this issue, plus ethical issues that pertain to human-subjects research. Based on experience with reproductive cloning in animals, the report concludes that human reproductive cloning would be dangerous for the woman, fetus, and newborn, and is likely to fail. The study panel did not address the issue of whether human reproductive cloning, even if it were found to be medically safe, would beâ€"or would not beâ€"acceptable to individuals or society.

The Future of Education Research at IES: Advancing an Equity-Oriented Science
publications.description: In 2002 Congress passed the Education Sciences Reform Act of 2002 (ESRA), authorizing the creation of the Institute of Education Sciences (IES) as the research, evaluation, statistics, and assessment arm of the Department of Education, and crystallizing the federal government's commitment to providing national leadership in expanding fundamental knowledge and understanding of education from early childhood through postsecondary study. IES shares information on the condition and progress of education in the United States, including early childhood education and special education; educational practices that support learning and improve academic achievement and access to educational opportunities for all students; and the effectiveness of federal and other education programs. In response to a request from the Institute of Education Sciences, this report provides guidance on the future of education research at the National Center for Education Research and the National Center for Special E
